forecasts.reasoning: Although the U.S. Supreme Court has ordered the U.S. government to facilitate Kilmar Abrego Garcia's return, he is currently held in an El Salvador maximum security prison (CECOT). Both the Trump administration (via AG Pam Bondi) and Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele have publicly and strongly refused to facilitate his return, arguing that he is under El Salvador's jurisdiction and will not be released. While the presiding federal judge (Paula Xinis) lifted a stay on discovery as of April 30 and ordered the government to produce sworn testimony in May regarding its efforts, the executive branch plans to resist these orders by invoking foreign policy privileges (as indicated by Secretary of State Marco Rubio). Since the U.S. judiciary cannot physically compel a foreign sovereign nation to release a prisoner, and given the protracted timeline typical of high-level constitutional and diplomatic disputes, it is highly improbable that this standoff will be resolved with Garcia returning to U.S. soil in the mere 60 days remaining before the July 1, 2025, deadline. The chance of resolution relies heavily on an unexpected diplomatic reversal, which seems very unlikely given the current political posturing of both administrations involved.

Multiple reports since January 2025 have consistently indicated that South Korea and Vietnam are in the 'final stages' of securing an agreement for the export of 20 K9 self-propelled howitzers. A late March 2025 report reiterates that Vietnam is 'set to purchase' the howitzers for around $276-$300 million. While some sources claim the deal was already signed, the most reliable phrasing indicates that the final contract is undergoing standard government-to-government bureaucracy and is pending an official signature or announcement. Given that the first units are expected to be delivered within 2025 (according to March 7 reports), the official signing is highly likely to take place in the near term. With two months left before the July 1, 2025 deadline, the probability of an official announcement is strong, though there remains a minor risk of bureaucratic delays pushing the formal signature into H2 2025.

The question requires the Global Protest Tracker to report a 10,000+ participant protest in South Korea with a start date AFTER the launch of the question (approx. mid-January 2025). While massive protests (up to 110,000 people) are currently taking place in Q1 2025, these are part of the movement triggered by President Yoon's martial law declaration in December 2024. The Global Protest Tracker groups protests by major 'episodes' or 'movements'. A recently published article by Carnegie (April 7, 2025) discusses the latest tracker update and explicitly links the South Korean mobilization to the December 2024 martial law trigger, indicating the start date will be recorded as December 2024, thereby failing the 'after launch' criterion. Furthermore, since the tracker just received an update in early April 2025, it is highly unlikely to be updated again with a brand new qualifying protest episode before the July 1, 2025 resolution deadline.

North Korea has not conducted a nuclear test since September 2017. While intelligence agencies have repeatedly released assessments since 2022 (including recently in March 2025) stating that North Korea has completed preparations at the Punggye-ri site and is ready to test 'on short notice,' these warnings have thus far described a state of physical readiness rather than an imminent political decision to act. Similar warnings peaked around the US election in late 2024, yet no test occurred. Given the short 2-month forecasting window (May 1 to July 1, 2025) and the absence of definitive, near-term triggers (such as new observable deployment activities that depart from the baseline of the past three years), the probability of a test occurring in this specific timeframe is exceptionally low. The historical base rate over the last eight years is 0%, and factoring in the possibility of sudden provocation yields a final estimate at the lowest bound of 5%.

The base rate of Russia launching an unprovoked invasion into a new country in any given 2-month window is extremely low (<1%), and practically 0% while its military is heavily bogged down in Ukraine. Searches confirm no current troop buildups, imminent threats, or ongoing diplomatic crises where the US is preparing such an accusation for countries like Georgia, Moldova, Kazakhstan, or any African states. In countries where Russian troops are recently deploying (Libya, Sahel region), they are doing so either with the consent of the ruling government/authorities or under conditions that the US does not formally classify as an "invasion" under the precise terms of the prompt. With only two months left until the resolution date and no indicators of a sudden geopolitical shift, the probability is at the minimum threshold.

As of Jan 2025, 47 countries had ratified AfCFTA, explicitly omitting Libya. While reports in Feb/March 2025 show 48 countries have now ratified, there is zero indication from multiple specific news searches that Libya was the 48th country. Furthermore, with only two months remaining until the July 1, 2025 deadline and Libya still facing severe political fragmentation between rival administrations, rapid ratification of a continental trade agreement appears highly improbable. Base rates for such actions in politically unstable countries in short time windows are near zero. I forecast broadly at the minimum probability boundary due to the total lack of evidence of ongoing legislative efforts or plans in Libya to ratify the agreement.

The event requires the U.S. government to publicly accuse Russia of invading any country other than Ukraine before July 1, 2025. This has not occurred yet (as of May 1, 2025). The timeline until the resolution date is extremely short (only two months). A new ground invasion by Russia is highly unlikely right now given its heavy military commitment and attrition in Ukraine. Furthermore, recent articles indicate that the U.S. (under the Trump administration in early 2025) has shifted towards a more conciliatory stance regarding Russia (even siding with Russia on a UN war resolution in Feb 2025). Therefore, the U.S. is very unlikely to proactively accuse Russia of a new invasion unless a glaring, undeniable military attack occurs. The absence of any reported troop buildups near countries like Moldova, Georgia, or the Baltic states provides strong evidence that no new invasion is imminent. Combining the very low base rate of such an occurrence within a two-month span with the current U.S. political orientation forms a solid basis for predicting practically a 2% chance.

The question requires the Global Protest Tracker to report a protest in South Korea with 10,000+ participants with a START DATE strictly after the launch of the question (mid-January 2025) and before July 1, 2025. Following Yoon's martial law declaration in December 2024, massive protests erupted. The tracker recently updated its data in April 2025 to include these South Korean protests. However, both the primary anti-Yoon protests and the pro-Yoon counter-protests began in December 2024. Since the Carnegie Global Protest Tracker typically groups protest waves by their originating event or campaign, the start date for this movement will almost certainly be recorded as December 2024. While there is a small chance that a completely new movement (e.g., labor strikes or post-impeachment verdict riots) triggers a brand new entry with a 2025 start date, the continuity of the current political crisis makes it highly likely that any ongoing massive rallies will simply be appended to the existing December 2024 tracker entry. Therefore, a NO resolution is very likely due to the strict start-date criterion.

The case of Kilmar Abrego Garcia features intense pressure from federal courts—including a Supreme Court order—demanding the U.S. government facilitate his return. Although the Trump administration and El Salvador's government initially stated he would not return, recent developments strongly push towards a YES resolution before July 1, 2025. By late April, the DOJ had developed a contingency plan to immediately deport him to Uganda upon his return to the U.S., offering the administration a face-saving way to comply with the court order without releasing him domestically. Furthermore, President Trump publicly acknowledged on April 29 that he could call President Bukele to have Abrego Garcia sent back. Returning him briefly to U.S. soil satisfies the resolution criteria, even if he is subsequently deported. 

The main risks for a NO resolution are the bureaucratic and legal delays that could drag the process past the 61-day window remaining until July 1, as the administration continues to battle discovery requests in court. Given the administration's stated contingency plans and Trump's latest remarks, a return followed by swift redeportation appears to be the most likely path to resolve the legal standoff, leading to a probabilistic lean toward YES.

Libya has not ratified the AfCFTA as of late April 2025, leaving only a two-month window before the July 1, 2025 deadline. Given Libya's severe, ongoing political fragmentation between rival governments in the east and west, passing and depositing the ratification of an international trade treaty requires a level of legislative and executive coordination that is currently entirely absent. Since there are absolutely no recent reports of legislative momentum or intent to ratify the agreement in the near term, the probability of this occurring within two months is extremely low.

Early Q1 2025 reports consistently indicated that South Korea and Vietnam were in the "final stages" of negotiating a $276-$300 million agreement for roughly 20 K9 self-propelled howitzers. While some defense blogs in March loosely claimed a contract was already "signed" or "selected", more authoritative mid-April reports from South Korean media (such as The Korea Herald on April 16) indicated the nations were still "reportedly in negotiations". This confirms the formal government announcement hasn't been finalized yet. However, the explicit high-level focus on defense exports during South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Tae-yul's mid-April visit to Hanoi signals strong political will to finalize the deal soon. Given the advanced stage of negotiations and intense diplomatic engagement, an announcement is substantially more likely than a standard baseline over a two-month timeframe, though typical procurement delays and bureaucratic hurdles in securing unprecedented arms sales slightly temper confidence.

North Korea has not conducted a nuclear test since 2017. While U.S. and South Korean intelligence continually assess that Pyongyang is 'ready to test on short notice' (a status they have maintained since completing tunnel preparations in 2022), there are no immediate signs of an imminent test as of May 1, 2025. Furthermore, the inauguration of Donald Trump in early 2025 shifts the geopolitical landscape; Kim Jong Un may prefer to leverage his existing arsenal for negotiations or diplomatic engagement rather than risk closing those doors with a highly provocative nuclear detonation. Given the extremely short forecasting window of just two months (until July 1, 2025) and the eight-year pause in testing, the base rate for a test occurring in this specific window is very low. With no acute intelligence warnings of an impending test, the probability remains firmly at the lower bound.
